items.q: Do most new business ideas come from brand-new inventions?
items.correct: No — most come from spotting gaps in existing markets, adapting existing products, or responding to changes in technology and consumer wants.
items.misconception: You can only start a business if you invent something completely new.
items.random_wrong: New business ideas are allocated to entrepreneurs by the government.
items.why: Theme 1 stresses opportunity-spotting; original ideas are rarely pure invention.

items.q: Is risk something entrepreneurs should always avoid?
items.correct: No — enterprise involves weighing risk against reward; successful entrepreneurs take calculated risks and reduce them through market research and planning.
items.misconception: Good entrepreneurs eliminate all risk before starting.
items.random_wrong: Business risk is automatically insured by banks.
items.why: Students treat risk as purely negative, missing the risk-reward trade-off at the heart of enterprise.

items.q: Does a business plan guarantee a start-up will succeed?
items.correct: No — a plan reduces risk by forcing research on customers, costs and cash flow, and helps obtain finance, but markets and assumptions can still change.
items.misconception: Writing a business plan ensures the business will work.
items.random_wrong: Business plans are legal contracts with customers.
items.why: Students overstate planning; its value is risk reduction and persuading lenders, not certainty.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Specific. “Strategic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Friedman. “Drucker” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: The real figure is 5 elements. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
